lessons.title: When and Why Merge Conflicts Occur
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to explain the conditions that cause merge conflicts and understand that fast-forward merges never conflict.
lessons.notes: Conflicts only in three-way merges. Require: (1) divergent histories, (2) same lines changed. Fast-forward merges skip this entirely.

lessons.title: Understanding Conflict Markers
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to interpret Git's conflict markers and understand what each section represents.
lessons.notes: Markers are literal text Git writes. Learn to read them: HEAD version above separator, incoming version below. Branch name on closing marker.

lessons.title: Identifying Conflicts with `git status` and `git diff`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to identify conflicted files and inspect them using Git commands.
lessons.notes: After failed merge, `git status` shows 'unmerged paths'. `git diff` shows conflict markers in context.

lessons.title: Manually Resolving a Conflict: Three Options
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to manually resolve a conflict by choosing to keep one version, the other, or a combination of both.
lessons.notes: Three choices: (1) keep HEAD version only, (2) keep incoming only, (3) write new code combining both. Remove markers and edit to create working state.

lessons.title: Completing a Merge: Staging and Committing
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to finalize a merge after resolving conflicts by staging resolved files and creating a merge commit.
lessons.notes: After editing files: (1) `git add` each resolved file, (2) `git commit` (Git pre-fills merge commit message). Now merge is complete.

lessons.title: Using Merge Strategies: `-X ours` and `-X theirs`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to use automatic merge strategies to favor one branch over another, and understand when this is appropriate.
lessons.notes: Strategies auto-resolve conflicts. Rare to use; usually better to decide manually based on domain knowledge. Use with caution.

lessons.title: Aborting a Merge You're Not Ready For
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to cancel a merge in progress without losing work.
lessons.notes: `git merge --abort` cancels the merge entirely and returns to pre-merge state. Safe, loses nothing. Use if you need time to think.

lessons.title: Practical Scenario: Real-World Conflict Resolution
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to apply conflict resolution skills to a realistic scenario with multiple files and logical decisions.
lessons.notes: Walk through a realistic example: two features edited same authentication module. Learner must decide which approach is best, or combine both thoughtfully.
